revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[it:Shikinen sengu]][[ja:神宮式年遷宮]][[zh:神宮式年遷宮]]
== Jingu Shikinen Sengu (Ise Grand Shrine Periodic Reconstruction) == [[File:Ise Shrine Naiku 1953-8-26 cropped.jpg|thumb|220px|The 59th Shikinen Sengu of the Naikū in 1953 (new shrine above, old shrine below)]] {{nihongo|'''Jingu Shikinen Sengu'''|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lit: Ise Grand Shrine Periodic Reconstruction}} refers to the regular *[[Sengū|sengu]]* (shrine reconstruction and relocation) performed at [[Ise Grand Shrine]] (*Jingu*). The *shikinen* (fixed cycle) is generally every 20 years.
== Overview == [[File:外宮別宮多賀宮新旧社殿.JPG|thumb|220x220px|New and old shrines of the Gekū Betsugū [[Taka-no-Miya|Tagagu Shrine]] built for the 62nd *Shikinen Sengu* (2013)]] [[File:Kiyonoibajinja sengu.jpg|thumb|Gekū (Toyōke Daijingū) [[:en:Sessha|Sessha]] [[Kiyonoiba Shrine]] on the day of *sengu* (2017)]] As a rule, every 20 years, the main sanctuaries of Naikū (*Kōtai Jingū*) and Gekū (*Toyōke Daijingū*), as well as all 14 associated [[:en:Betsugū|Betsugū]] (*subordinate shrines*), are completely rebuilt, and the divine spirits (*shinza*) are relocated to the new structures. Along with the main sanctuaries, 65 other shrine buildings, including the *Hōden Gaheiden* (*treasury and outer hall*), *Torii* gates, *Mikaki* fences, and *Mikeden* (*food offering halls*), are reconstructed. Additionally, 1,576 sacred garments and treasures (*gosōshoku shinpō*), such as ceremonial robes and {{ill|Sugari no Ontachi|en|Sugari no Ontachi|qid=Q7634986|lt=Sugari no Ontachi|1=Sugari no Ontachi}} (*divine swords*), are newly made<ref name="About Sengu">{{Cite web|url=https://www.isejingu.or.jp/sengu/aboutsengu.html |title=About Sengu|website= Ise Grand Shrine|accessdate=2022-08-21}}</ref>. Furthermore, the [[Ujibashi (Ise City)|Uji Bridge]] is also reconstructed<ref group="Note">The tradition of rebuilding Uji Bridge as part of the *Shikinen Sengu* began during the Meiji period.</ref><ref group="Note" name="4year">The *Shikinen Sengu* originally planned for 1949 was delayed by four years due to World War II. However, since the Uji Bridge had deteriorated significantly, it was rebuilt after 20 years as originally planned. Since then, the Uji Bridge has continued to be reconstructed four years ahead of each *Shikinen Sengu*.</ref>.
Historical records indicate that the *Jingu Shikinen Sengu* was established by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|qid=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, and the first reconstruction was carried out in the 4th year of {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}'s reign (690)<ref name="About Sengu"/>. Despite interruptions of over 120 years during the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]] and several postponements, the tradition has continued for approximately 1,300 years, with the 62nd *Shikinen Sengu* taking place in 2013.
The preparations for the 62nd *Shikinen Sengu* began in 2005, with the key ritual, *Ujiwatari Hajimeshiki* (*first crossing of Uji Bridge*), conducted in 2009. The main *sengu* (*transfer of the divine body*) took place in 2013. According to the [[Jingu Administration Office]], the total cost of the 62nd *Shikinen Sengu*, including construction, clothing, and treasures, amounted to approximately 55 billion yen. Of this amount, 33 billion yen came from Ise Grand Shrine’s funds, while 22 billion yen was covered by donations<ref>「全国10万社の頂点に鎮座 比類なき伊勢神宮の威力」『[[Weekly Diamond]]』April 16, 2016 issue</ref>.
One reason Ise Grand Shrine has not been registered as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} is that the *Shikinen Sengu* is seen as incompatible with the criteria of "permanence" and "preservation" required for World Heritage recognition<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/entry/index.php?id=1000312213&page=ref_view#:~:text=*%EF%BD%90178%20%E4%B8%96%E7%95%8C%E9%81%BA%E7%94%A3%E3%81%93%E3%81%BC%E3%82%8C%E8%A9%B1%E3%80%8C%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2,%E3%81%8C%E6%8E%A1%E3%82%89%E3%82%8C%E3%81%A6%E3%81%84%E3%81%BE%E3%81%99%E3%80%82 |publisher=Reference Cooperative Database|title=Why is Ise Grand Shrine not a World Heritage Site?|accessdate=2022-08-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.barifuri.com/tourcenter/mame/12_sekaiisan.html |title=Why Ise Grand Shrine Cannot Become a World Heritage Site|publisher=|accessdate=2022-08-21}}</ref>.
== Significance == [[File:Ise Shrine Geku Sengyo-no-gi 1953.jpg|thumb|The *Sengyo no Gi* (*transfer of the deity*) at Gekū on October 5, 1953 (first time the ceremony was photographed)]] [[File:Ise Shrine Geku (Old shrine).jpg|thumb|The old Gekū shrine after the 2013 *Shikinen Sengu* (photo taken in March 2014)]] The *Shikinen Sengu* is performed because the shrine structures are built as [[pit-dwelling pillar structures]] with [[thatch roofs]]. Unlike buildings with foundation stones, the untreated cypress pillars are directly embedded into the ground, making them more vulnerable to decay. Consequently, after a fixed period, an identical replica of the original Yayoi-style structure is constructed.
Although the use of lacquer for wood preservation dates back to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], and stone foundations had already been developed by the 7th century when Emperor Tenmu established the *Shikinen Sengu*, Ise Grand Shrine continued using ancient building techniques. The contemporaneous construction of [[:en:Horyu-ji|Horyu-ji]], the world's oldest wooden structure, shows that stone-based structures were available, but Ise’s approach was intentionally maintained for its spiritual significance.
The *Shikinen Sengu* Public Relations Office (2013) explained that the main reason for rebuilding is the belief in *Tokowaka* (*eternal renewal*), which keeps the divine presence fresh and vibrant<ref name="Nikkei20121117">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGKDZO48500750W2A111C1BC8000/ "Sengu: Renewal for Eternity – Ise Grand Shrine and Izumo Taisha in 2013"] 『[[:en:Nikkei Shimbun|Nikkei Shimbun]]』 November 17, 2012 (Culture Section), retrieved August 21, 2022</ref>. The 20-year cycle, recorded in the [[Engishiki]], has various interpretations, including the shelf life of stored *hoshi* (dried rice), the sacred nature of the number 20, ancient calendar systems, and the need for carpenters and treasure artisans to pass down their skills<ref name="Nikkei20121117"/>.
== Building Materials == [[File:Akasawa Natural Recreational Forest-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|1985 logging site for the Ise Grand Shrine *Shikinen Sengu* in the [[Akasawa Natural Recreational Forest]], {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}]] Over 10,000 *hinoki* (Japanese cypress) logs are used for each *sengu*, sourced from sacred forests known as *Misomayama* (*divine lumber mountains*). Initially, all timber came from forests near the shrine, but by the 14th century, materials began to be sourced from *Mikawa* (for Naikū) and *Mino* (for Gekū). By the 47th *Shikinen Sengu* in the Edo period, logging had moved to {{ill|Kiso Valley|en|Kiso Valley|qid=Q6416831|lt=Kiso Valley|1=Kiso Valley}} due to timber depletion.
A systematic reforestation effort began in the Meiji era, with the establishment of *Jingu forests* in {{ill|Kiso|en|Kiso|qid=Q355726|lt=Kiso|1=Kiso}}, ensuring sustainable timber production over 200-300 years. For the first time in 700 years, the 2013 *Shikinen Sengu* used thinned wood from the sacred forests around the shrine, covering 25% of the materials used.
Additionally, *Jingu*-owned forests in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} and {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} are expected to become future sources of shrine timber<ref>『遷宮論集』「神宮域林の用材生産計画」, p. 479</ref>.
== References ==
